message.content: Here's a weird candidate for a heat BD pace: I'm musing installing a step at _orient via mount or groom that touches the gazette input markdown file, so that we get the 'write input file' permission out of the way at the top of the heat.  Thoughts?  Are you aware of this phenomenon? Research codebase and websearch for awareness.

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Investigate step for handling heat BD pace input

content.text: This is a sharp ergonomics question. Let me research it properly — both the codebase (how the gazette write actually triggers the permission, and whether anything already addresses it) and the web (how Claude Code's permission model handles "pre-approving" a path). Let me kick off both in parallel.

Based on the search results, I found information about Claude Code's permission prompts and the "don't ask again" feature:

## Key Findings

When you approve a compound command with "Yes, don't ask again", Claude Code saves a separate rule for each subcommand that requires approval, rather than a single rule for the full compound string.

Every time you click "Yes, and don't ask again" it appends an entry to your settings file, specifically `settings.local.json` according to the documentation.

## Permission Scope and Session Settings

The permission rules you create through "don't ask again" are stored in configuration files and persist across sessions. You can configure allow, deny, and ask rules declaratively in .claude/settings.json. The SDK does not load filesystem settings by default, so you must set setting_sources=["project"] for these rules to apply.

## How Rules Work Per File Path

Rules evaluate in order: deny first, then ask, then allow. The first matching rule wins, so deny rules always take precedence. This means your Write tool permissions can be scoped to specific file paths using pattern matching.

The search results indicate that the permission system supports wildcard patterns and specific path rules in `settings.json` to control which operations are auto-approved versus which still require prompts on a per-path basis.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Claude Code's permission "don't ask again" feature and settings persistence:

## Persistence Issues with "Don't Ask Again"

When selecting "Yes, and don't ask again for [command] in [project]" during a permission prompt, the setting does not persist across sessions despite having the permission already saved in .claude/settings.local.json.

## Problems with Directory/Path-Based Rules

Users have reported that despite setting "don't ask again for similar commands" in a specific project directory, Claude continues to ask for permission for commands like find and grep repeatedly. This suggests that path-based glob rules in `settings.local.json` may not persist consistently when dealing with directory-specific permissions.

## Known Bugs

There are multiple reported issues with persistence:

- After the Edit tool modifies settings.local.json, the in-memory permission state appears to get out of sync with the file on disk, suggesting Claude Code's permission cache doesn't reload after an external write.

- Even when users select the "Yes, and don't ask again for similar commands" option, Claude seems to get amnesia and asks again the very next time.

## Rule Path Glob Specifics

Wildcard patterns (*) don't always match as expected, and exact command matching may be required.

The search results indicate these are recognized bugs that Anthropic is aware of, but comprehensive fixes have been slow to roll out.

message.content: I am not asking for any sort of preconfiguration of permissions; this has never worked very well.  What I _am_ asking for is a step during groom and mount that trigger it so that I can personally grant the permission as part of chat beginning ceremony?

content.text:
Got it — you want the prompt *hoisted to ceremony time*, not configured away. That reframes it, and I now understand the phenomenon precisely. Here's what the research turned up and my honest read.

## Yes — and here's the exact mechanism

Your `settings.local.json` blanket-allows `Read` and `Edit` but **not `Write`** (1705 allow entries, zero `Write(...)` rules, zero gazette/jjm *write* rules — only Bash entries that happen to mention the path). Combine that with two JJK design facts:

1. The gazette lives at `.claude/jjm/officia/<session-id>/gazette_in.md`, where `<session-id>` is minted fresh per chat by `jjx_open`.
2. Every jjx call **deletes both gazette files on entry**. So the agent can never *Edit* an existing gazette_in.md — it must *Write* (create) it fresh each time.

That's the whole phenomenon in one line: **the delete-on-entry lifetime forces `Write`, and `Write` is the one tool you didn't blanket-allow.** So the first gazette setter of each chat (slate / reslate / paddock-set) trips a `Write` prompt — and it lands mid-flow, exactly when you're trying to approve a docket.

The saving grace your idea exploits: that path is **stable for the whole chat** (one officium per `jjx_open`), so a single grant covers every subsequent gazette write that session.

## The proposal works, with one load-bearing detail

A throwaway write to the current officium's `gazette_in.md` at ceremony start fires the *identical* permission gate as the real writes, so granting it early hoists the friction to a calm moment. It's safe — the next jjx call deletes the placeholder, so there's no stale-content hazard.

**The detail that makes or breaks it: it must be the `Write` tool, not a `Bash(touch ...)`.** Permission scope is per-tool. A Bash touch grants a Bash permission; the real gazette writes use `Write`. Only a `Write`-tool pre-touch grants the matching gate.

## Where to hang it — my one disagreement

You said "mount and groom." I'd split them:

- **Groom — strong fit.** Grooming *is* the slate/reslate/paddock-edit ceremony. The gate gets used almost every time.
- **Mount — weak fit.** A pure execution mount (orient → work → notch → wrap) *never* writes a gazette: `jjx_record` and `jjx_close` are param-based, and even the mount-protocol rename step (`jjx_relabel`) is param-based. So a mount pre-touch generates a grant you often won't use — a prompt for nothing.

Cleaner anchor than either: **`jjx_open` / chat-start itself**, since the officium is per-chat and one pre-touch covers the entire session regardless of what you do next. That matches your own words — "as part of chat beginning ceremony" — better than tying it to mount/groom.

## Two caveats from the research

- **Verify the grant actually suppresses the *next* gazette write before trusting it.** Multiple open issues show "don't ask again" persistence is flaky ([#21080](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/21080), [#29400](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/29400), [#11073](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/11073)). My read is those are mostly *cross-session* and *Bash-command-matching* failures, so a within-session file-`Write` grant should hold — but if the bug bites here, the pre-touch buys nothing, so it's worth one empirical confirmation. The flakiness actually *reinforces* your stance: don't rely on persisted config, grant per-chat as ceremony ([config docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions)).
- **I couldn't find this as a named community pattern.** "Deliberately Write a throwaway file to hoist a permission prompt to a convenient time" isn't documented anywhere I searched — it's a local invention. A reasonable one, but you're not copying a known idiom.

## The road not taken (so you can reject it on purpose)

Because `Edit` *is* allowed and `Write` isn't, the friction would vanish entirely if `gazette_in.md` **persisted** instead of being deleted each call — you'd `Edit` a standing file, no prompt ever. But that trades away the deliberate single-MCP-call lifetime ("a parameter or a return value, not persistent state"), which is load-bearing against stale-gazette poisoning. Your ceremony pre-touch respects the existing design; this alternative removes the friction but spends a real invariant to do it. I'd keep the invariant.

---

Net: it's a real phenomenon, the trick is mechanically sound, and the per-chat framing is the right one. I'd anchor it at officium-open (or groom), make it a `Write`-tool touch, and validate the in-session suppression once.

Want me to pull up ₣BD's shape to judge whether this belongs there or wants its own home — or draft the docket?

Sources: [Configure permissions](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions) · [#21080](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/21080) · [#29400](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/29400) · [#11073](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/11073)

content.text:
I want to push back on this one, clearly — because I think it solves the permission problem by spending something more valuable.

## What the gazette channel actually *is*

Look at which commands take gazette input today: `jjx_enroll`, `jjx_redocket`, `jjx_paddock`-set. The common thread isn't "they're important" or "they run at ceremony" — it's that **they carry content too rich/multiline for JSON params**: docket bodies, paddock prose. Every other command, including every identity-keyed one, takes its key as a param. The organizing principle is explicit in your own guidance: *"When a command takes a firemark or coronet, provide the identity, not the silks"* — identities go in params; the gazette is *"the sole input path for docket content."*

A firemark is the exact opposite of gazette-shaped content. It's a 2-char identity. Routing it through the gazette means a `# jjezs_orient ₣BD` notice with **no body** — a wire format built for "header + body" carrying only a header. That's a degenerate use of the channel, and it inverts the one clean line JJK currently holds: *getters take identities via params; setters take content via gazette.*

## The deeper objection: you'd be hiding a permission workaround inside a domain interface

The permission friction is a *harness* fact — Claude Code's per-tool `Write` gate plus your settings. The gazette I/O contract is a *JJK domain* fact. Coupling them means a JJK interface decision becomes load-bearing for a Claude Code permission behavior.

Run it through your own load-bearing test. Is firemark-in-gazette load-bearing? After the redesign, yes — remove it and orient breaks. But that's circular: you *manufactured* the dependency. The *underlying intent* of orient is "tell me about ₣BD," which a param firemark satisfies completely. The gazette routing adds zero intent-fidelity; it only adds permission-timing as a side effect. That's the textbook non-load-bearing trap — *cognitive cost up, correctness flat.*

And it's worse than the throwaway pre-touch in one specific way: the throwaway is *obviously* a workaround. Firemark-in-gazette would *look like an interface choice*. A future maintainer (or a different MCP client — the JJK server isn't Claude-Code-specific) sees "orient reads its firemark from gazette" with no signal that it exists to hoist a permission prompt. The Pale discipline says a workaround must "carry a removal condition." This one would shed its removal condition the moment it's written — if you ever blanket-allow `Write`, the coupling stays, now pure overhead nobody recognizes as vestigial.

## Plus a concrete tax

`jjx_orient` and `jjx_show` are your hottest read commands — every mount, groom, parade. Today `jjx_show {target:"BD"}` is one cheap call. Under this, every navigation read becomes *write gazette_in, then call* — doubling tool calls on the most frequent operations, to serve a once-per-chat permission grant.

## The root mismatch

Mount and groom are **reads**. Reads don't have input bodies. Any "required write" you bolt onto a read-shaped operation will be contrived — not because you designed it badly, but because the operation genuinely has nothing for a writer to supply. That's why every attempt here feels like it's missing content: there *is* no natural content.

## Where I'd actually land

You've correctly rejected preconfig. Given that, the honest options are only two:

1. **Accept the one mid-flow prompt.** It's once per chat. Maybe not worth a structural change.
2. **The labeled throwaway at officium-open** — but framed correctly as a *membrane*, not dressed up as a feature: a `Write`-tool touch of `gazette_in.md` right after `jjx_open`, documented in CLAUDE.md as *"permission-hoist for the Claude Code `Write` gate; delete when `Write` is blanket-allowed or the harness gains pre-grant."* That keeps the workaround at the layer where the quirk lives (the agent's ceremony), with its removal condition intact, instead of pushing it down into the JJK interface where it calcifies.

My recommendation: **don't route the firemark through the gazette.** If the prompt timing bothers you enough to act, do the labeled membrane at open. If it doesn't quite, do nothing — one prompt per chat is cheap, and the interface stays honest.

Where's your weight — is the mid-flow prompt actually costing you enough to warrant the membrane, or is this more "it offends the sense of order that the write isn't pre-cleared"? Those want different answers.

message.content: This time I override your objection.  Usability trumps getter setter orthodoxy here.  Too many chats get stalled without me noticing I need to grant this permission after a few turns; groom and heat clearing this is usability.  Given my decision, any more questions before we slate such a pace?

content.text:
Override accepted — and it's a fair one. A permission you don't notice you need, that silently stalls a chat several turns deep, is a worse failure than spending some getter/setter orthodoxy. Lived usability beats a clean diagram here. I'll record the rationale as a `## Cinched` note in the docket so a future instance reads it as a deliberate trade, not an accident to "fix."

A few things I'm *not* asking — they're mount-time mechanical: minting the new input slug(s) (e.g. `jjezs_orient`), discovering every caller of these commands, and the spec/CLAUDE.md doc updates that ride along. Those follow the rules without your input.

But two forks genuinely change the docket's cinched constraints and blast radius, and they're yours to call:

questions.question: Which read commands should route heat selection through the gazette (so the Write permission fires when you call them)?

options.label: Mount + groom only
options.description: jjx_orient (mount) and the heat-overview form of jjx_show (groom). Single-pace parade — jjx_show {target: coronet} — stays param-based, so quick pace reads stay cheap. Hits the two ceremony entry points without taxing every casual read.

options.label: Mount + all jjx_show
options.description: jjx_orient plus EVERY jjx_show, including single-pace parade. Most uniform rule, but every read of any pace now requires a gazette write first — the heaviest tax.

options.label: Mount only
options.description: jjx_orient only. Groom's jjx_show stays unchanged. Narrowest blast radius, but grooming sessions that never mount won't get the early prompt.

questions.question: How strictly should the gazette requirement be enforced on those commands?

options.label: Hard — gazette is sole input
options.description: The command rejects a param firemark; the write is unavoidable, so the prompt ALWAYS fires at ceremony. Directly answers your pain (soft conventions get missed unnoticed). Cost: any programmatic/test caller of these commands must be updated to write the gazette first — mount agent reconciles this.

options.label: Soft — param fallback retained
options.description: Gazette accepted and the mount/groom protocol mandates it, but a param firemark still works. Automation and quick parades never break — but the ceremony guarantee leans on the protocol being followed, which is exactly the kind of thing that currently slips.

content.text:
Genuinely glad you shared it — and I'm going to push back on your "FYI only," because I think this screenshot is load-bearing for the pace, not incidental.

Look at the options the harness offered:

1. Yes
2. Yes, and allow Claude to edit its own settings for this session
3. No

There's **no "Yes, and don't ask again for Writes to this path/directory"** — the granular session-scoped allow we were implicitly counting on. My read: because the gazette lives under `.claude/`, the harness treats any write there as *settings-adjacent* and gates it more conservatively, so the only persistence offer is the blunt "let Claude edit its own settings."

That matters for the pace's whole premise. The payoff we designed for is "grant once at ceremony → covered for the rest of the chat." But if plain **Yes (option 1) is one-time**, the ceremony hoist would *add* a prompt at the top without removing the mid-flow ones — net worse.
